steps.title: Identify the key finding and what it must suggest
steps.content: Valerio and colleagues found that participants recruited via snowball sampling (recruited by people in their own social networks) had a much higher retention rate than participants recruited by strangers. The blank must explain why social-network recruitment improves retention — the answer must logically follow from that specific contrast.

steps.title: Test each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'being recruited to participate in a study by someone with whom one is socially connected may impart a feeling of obligation to persist with participation in the study.': This directly explains why social-network recruits stay — the social connection creates a sense of obligation. That’s a logical inference from the higher retention rate among people recruited by someone they know. ✓
  • Choice B — 'people with relatively small social networks are inherently less likely to be recruited to participate in a study via snowball sampling than are people with relatively large social networks.': This addresses who gets recruited, not why recruits stay once in the study. Retention is the key finding, not recruitment likelihood. ✗
  • Choice C — 'snowball sampling is more likely to improve retention rates among rural participants than among nonrural participants.': The study only examined rural counties; no nonrural comparison was made, so this rural-vs-nonrural conclusion is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — 'social networks can become large enough that two people can share a network but nevertheless regard each other as strangers.': This contradicts the finding — it implies social-network recruits might be strangers anyway, which would undermine rather than explain the retention advantage. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
